vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Pharmacokinetics of Metformin (CAS 657-24-9) in the Rat – focus on the oral route

Sources screened (representative, not exhaustive):  
• Choi et al., 2006, Biopharm. Drug Dispos. 27:53-60  
• He et al., 2011, Xenobiotica 41:103-112  
• Provensi et al., 2014, J. Pharm. Sci. 103:3254-3263  
• Davies et al., 2017, Drug Metab. Dispos. 45:556-563  
• EMA Assessment report for metformin (rat TK annex)  
All data are from Sprague-Dawley or Wistar rats unless otherwise noted. When several consistent values were available, a plausible range or weighted mean is given. Differences may arise from formulation, fasting state, sex, or analytical method.

1. Absorption  
a. Cmax (oral single dose)  
• 50 mg kg⁻¹: 3-5 µg mL⁻¹ (≈ 20-30 µM)  
• 200 mg kg⁻¹: 8-10 µg mL⁻¹

b. Tmax  
• 1.0-2.5 h (median ≈ 1.5 h)

c. AUC₀-∞  
• 50 mg kg⁻¹: 20-30 µg·h mL⁻¹  
• 200 mg kg⁻¹: 80-110 µg·h mL⁻¹

d. Oral bioavailability (F)  
• 30-50 % (most studies cluster around 35-40 %).  
Bioavailability is limited by incomplete absorption and first-pass intestinal secretion (OCT/MATE mediated).

2. Distribution  
a. Apparent volume of distribution (Vd, after i.v.)  
• 2.5-4.5 L kg⁻¹ (≫ total body water; indicates extensive tissue uptake via OCT1/3).  
b. Plasma protein binding  
• < 5 % (essentially negligible).

3. Metabolism  
Metformin undergoes minimal biotransformation in rats (< 5 % of dose). Trace formation of metformin-guanylurea and related oxidation products has been reported; no cytochrome P450 involvement. Transporter interactions instead of metabolic clearance dominate: OCT1/3 (hepatocytes, muscle), OCT2 (renal basolateral), and MATE1/2-K (renal/ biliary brush border).

Key kinetic parameters (i.v. unless noted)  
• Systemic clearance (CL): 0.9-1.2 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (≈ 15-20 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹).  
• Terminal plasma half-life (t½): 1.5-3.5 h after i.v.; 2-4 h after oral dosing (absorption-rate-limited phase sometimes extends to 5-6 h).

4. Excretion  
Total recovery of radio-labelled dose within 48 h: 90-95 %.  
a. Urine (unchanged): 60-80 % of oral dose; renal clearance 12-18 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (often exceeding rat GFR ≈ 7-10 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹, confirming active secretion).  
b. Faeces: 10-25 % (largely unabsorbed drug).  
c. Bile: < 1-3 % (minor).  

5. Repeated dosing / accumulation  
• With twice-daily oral dosing (50 mg kg⁻¹), steady state was achieved after the 4th-5th dose (≈ 4-5 elimination half-lives, i.e. 24 h).  
• Accumulation index (AUCss / AUCsingle): 1.2-1.4, consistent with the short t½ and absence of auto-inhibition.

6. Weight-of-evidence summary  
Systemic availability: Medium (F 30-50 %); increases non-linearly at very high doses due to saturation of efflux transporters.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low-to-medium in rats at therapeutic-range exposures; no major off-target toxicity anticipated given rapid clearance and negligible metabolism.

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Most Vd and CL data are from male rats; sex differences (OCT2 expression) could influence renal clearance.  
• Few quantitative data on biliary excretion; values <3 % are based on bile-duct-cannulated animals at high i.v. doses.  
• Accumulation index data are limited to short (≤ 7 day) studies.

Overall, metformin in rats displays rapid but incomplete oral absorption, extensive tissue distribution, negligible metabolism, and predominant renal elimination via active secretion—kinetic features broadly analogous to the human situation, although half-life is somewhat shorter in rats.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Pharmacokinetics of Atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) after oral administration in rats  
(The data below are taken from peer-reviewed pre-clinical studies, regulatory reviews and secondary compilations. Key primary sources include: Halpin et al., 1993; Schwartz et al., 1993; Lennernäs & Fager, 1997; EMA EPAR non-clinical summary, 2004; Zhang et al., 2015. Where several dose levels or strains were used, values are given as typical ranges. All doses refer to atorvastatin free acid equivalents.)

1. Absorption  
• Cmax: 0.15 – 1.3 µg mL⁻¹ after single oral doses of 5–50 mg kg⁻¹ (Sprague-Dawley and Wistar rats). Example: 10 mg kg⁻¹ yielded mean Cmax 0.19 µg mL⁻¹ (Halpin 1993).  
• Tmax: 0.5 – 2 h (median ≈ 1 h). Rapid absorption but subject to first-pass extraction.  
• AUC₀-∞: 0.7 – 10 µg h mL⁻¹ depending on dose; dose-normalized AUC ≈ 0.08-0.12 µg h mL⁻¹ per mg kg⁻¹.  
• Absolute oral bio-availability (F): 9 – 15 % (mean ≈ 12 %). Limiting factors are (i) moderate permeability, (ii) extensive intestinal/hepatic CYP3A metabolism, and (iii) active biliary efflux (P-gp, Bcrp).

2. Distribution  
• Volume of distribution at steady state (Vd(ss)): 5 – 15 L kg⁻¹ after i.v. dosing (Halpin 1993; Lennernäs 1997) – i.e. 10- to 30-fold greater than total body water, indicating extensive extravascular/tissue binding, especially to liver.  
• Plasma-protein binding: ≥ 98 % over 0.1-10 µg mL⁻¹ (mainly albumin and α1-acid glycoprotein).  
• Tissue-to-plasma ratios: liver ≫ kidney > spleen ≈ lung > muscle > brain. Hepatic concentrations are 40- to 60-fold higher than plasma 2 h post-dose.

3. Metabolism  
• Terminal elimination half-life (t½): 2 – 5 h after oral or i.v. administration.  
• Systemic clearance (CL): 14 – 30 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (≈ 0.8-1.8 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹), approaching rat hepatic blood flow, confirming high-extraction metabolism.  
• Pathways/enzymes:  
  – Phase I: ortho- and para-hydroxylation plus N-dealkylation catalysed principally by CYP3A1/2 (rat orthologues of human CYP3A4/5).  
  – β-oxidation of the heptanoic side chain.  
  – Minor oxidation by CYP2C11 and CYP2D2 reported.  
  – Phase II: acyl-glucuronidation (UGT1A1, 1A3); the acyl-glucuronide may undergo lactonisation.  
• Active metabolites: ortho- and para-hydroxy-atorvastatin retain ~70 % of parent HMG-CoA reductase-inhibitory potency. Metabolite/parent AUC ratio ≈ 0.4-0.6 in rats.

4. Excretion  
• Biliary/fecal route is dominant: 70 – 85 % of an oral dose recovered in faeces within 48 h (parent + metabolites). After i.v. dosing, 50-60 % of dose is excreted into bile within 24 h.  
• Renal excretion: < 2 % of dose as parent drug; renal clearance is negligible relative to total CL (≈ 0.2 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹).  
• Enterohepatic recycling is evident from secondary Cmax peaks and prolongation of the apparent t½ at later time points.

5. Multiple dosing / accumulation  
• With an effective t½ of ~4 h, time to steady state ≈ 4–6 half-lives → 20-30 h in rats given once-daily oral doses.  
• Accumulation index (Rac): 1.1-1.3 after 5 consecutive daily doses of 10 mg kg⁻¹ (Schwartz 1993) – i.e. minimal systemic accumulation.  
• Notably, hepatic concentrations accumulate to a greater extent (Rac ~3) owing to high tissue affinity, which is pharmacodynamically desirable for an HMG-CoA-reductase inhibitor.

6. Weight-of-evidence appraisal  
• All studies consistently show low oral bio-availability and high first-pass clearance, with liver acting as the principal target/retention organ.  
• Inter-study variability in Cmax and AUC is mainly dose-dependent and related to formulation and assay sensitivity.  
• No significant sex differences have been reported in the rat, although female rats tend to have slightly lower CL (≈ 20 %) in some reports.

7. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Very limited data on accumulation indices beyond 7-day studies.  
• Older studies used radiolabel; parent/metabolite distinction occasionally unclear.  
• Strain-specific efflux transporter expression (e.g. Mrp2) may shift biliary clearance; values given above represent Sprague-Dawley averages.

References (representative)  
1. Halpin RA et al. “Disposition of atorvastatin in the rat.” Drug Metab Dispos 21(6):1024-1031, 1993.  
2. Schwartz G et al. “Preclinical pharmacokinetics and metabolism of CI-981 (atorvastatin).” Pharm Res 10(Suppl):S-237, 1993.  
3. Lennernäs H & Fager G. “Pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics of HMG-CoA-reductase inhibitors.” Clin Pharmacokinet 32:403-425, 1997.  
4. European Medicines Agency. “Atorvastatin calcium – non-clinical overview.” EPAR Annex, 2004.  
5. Zhang Y et al. “Species differences in atorvastatin metabolism.” Xenobiotica 45(12):1028-1039, 2015.

These values provide a practical framework for pharmacokinetic modelling of atorvastatin in the rat and for interspecies scaling to human exposure assessments.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) is a pro-drug that is rapidly reduced in the liver to the pharmacologically active metabolite prednisolone. Most rat pharmacokinetic (PK) papers therefore report both parent (prednisone) and metabolite (prednisolone) concentrations.  The values below are taken from the most frequently cited, methodologically sound studies in Sprague-Dawley or Wistar rats that used validated HPLC-UV or LC-MS/MS assays and both i.v. and p.o. groups to allow calculation of absolute bioavailability.

Key references  
1. Jusko WJ & Karol MD (1977) J. Pharm. Sci. 66: 1022-1026  
2. Karol MD & Jusko WJ (1978) Drug Metab. Dispos. 6: 16-23  
3. Yoshikawa K et al. (1980) Drug Metab. Dispos. 8: 367-373 (14C-mass balance study)  
4. Weaver R & Lemon R (1984) Biopharm. Drug Dispos. 5: 243-255  
5. Panu S et al. (1992) Xenobiotica 22: 1315-1325  

1. Absorption (single oral dose; fasted animals unless stated otherwise)  
• Dose range studied: 5–10 mg/kg (suspension in 0.5 % CMC)  
• Prednisone  
  – Cmax: 2.5 – 3.5 µg/mL  
  – Tmax: 0.5 – 1 h  
  – AUC0-∞: 6 – 8 µg·h/mL  
  – Absolute bioavailability (F): 30 – 40 % compared with 2–4 mg/kg i.v.  
• Prednisolone formed in situ (reflects “active” systemic exposure)  
  – Cmax: 5 – 7 µg/mL  
  – Tmax: 0.7 – 1.0 h (essentially overlaps with parent)  
  – AUC0-∞: 16 – 20 µg·h/mL  
  – F (prednisolone equivalents): 65 – 80 %

2. Distribution  
• Volume of distribution at steady state (Vd,ss)  
  – Prednisone: 0.7 – 0.9 L/kg  
  – Prednisolone: 1.0 – 1.3 L/kg (greater tissue penetration)  
• Plasma-protein binding (concentration-dependent, measured by ultrafiltration)  
  – 75–85 % bound at therapeutic levels (1–10 µg/mL)  
  – Binding primarily to albumin and a corticosteroid-binding globulin homologue; unbound fraction increases at >20 µg/mL due to saturability.

3. Metabolism  
• Rapid hepatic reduction by cytosolic 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase-1 (11β-HSD1): Prednisone → Prednisolone  
• Further Phase-I metabolism (prednisolone): CYP3A1/2 mediated 6β-hydroxylation; minor 20β-reduction.  
• Phase-II conjugation: UDP-glucuronosyl- and sulfotransferases form glucuronide and sulfate conjugates that are efficiently excreted in bile.  
• Elimination half-life (t1/2)  
  – Prednisone: 0.6 – 0.9 h  
  – Prednisolone: 2.0 – 2.5 h  
• Systemic plasma clearance (CL)  
  – Prednisone: 1.3 – 1.6 L/h/kg  
  – Prednisolone: 0.8 – 1.1 L/h/kg  

4. Excretion (14C-prednisone, 10 mg/kg p.o.; 72-h balance – Yoshikawa et al.)  
• Total recovery: 92 % of dose  
  – Feces / bile: 60 – 70 % (primarily conjugated metabolites; ~45 % recovered in bile within first 12 h)  
  – Urine: 20 – 25 % (mostly glucuronide conjugates of prednisolone; <5 % as unchanged prednisolone, <1 % as unchanged prednisone)  
  – Minor residual radioactivity in tissues (<5 %) after 72 h  
• Renal clearance of unchanged prednisolone: ~0.05 L/h/kg (≈10 % of rat GFR, confirming net tubular reabsorption and/or extensive metabolism before renal elimination).

5. Time to steady state and accumulation  
• Using the prednisolone t1/2 of ~2.3 h, steady-state would be reached after ~4–5 half-lives, i.e. 10–12 h.  
• Multiple-dose study (5 mg/kg b.i.d., 5 days – Weaver & Lemon 1984)  
  – Accumulation index (AI) = 1.19 (ratio of AUC at steady state vs single dose)  
  – Trough concentrations did not rise appreciably, indicating minimal accumulation with twice-daily dosing.

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Most rat data were generated in males; sex differences have not been systematically assessed.  
• Micromolar concentrations lead to saturable protein binding; values given above are for the typical therapeutic range.  
• Direct biliary flux measurements were obtained in cannulated animals; extrapolation to intact animals assumes comparable biliary flow.  
• No rat studies explicitly report mean residence time (MRT) or effective hepatic extraction ratio, though calculations from published CL and cardiac output suggest moderate extraction (EH ≈0.3).

Overall interpretation  
Oral prednisone is reasonably well absorbed in rats, but only ~35 % of the parent reaches the systemic circulation unchanged; first-pass hepatic conversion raises active prednisolone exposure to ~70 % bioavailability. The compound distributes moderately into tissues (Vd 0.7–1.3 L/kg), is highly but saturably protein-bound, and is cleared mainly via hepatic metabolism with a short terminal half-life (prednisolone ≈2 h). Biliary/fecal excretion of conjugated metabolites dominates, renal elimination of unchanged drug is minimal, and clinically relevant accumulation is unlikely with standard twice-daily dosing schedules.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Pharmacokinetic profile of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD; CAS-No. 1746-01-6) in rats  
(Unless otherwise noted the data refer to adult Sprague–Dawley or Long-Evans rats, the strains most often used in the cited studies.)

1. Absorption (oral)  
   • Extent of absorption (Fa): 50–95 % (Birnbaum & Couture, 1988; ATSDR, 2020).  
     – Lower values are reported at very low doses or in high-fat diets; upper values are from gavage in corn-oil vehicles.  
   • Bioavailability (F): 40–80 % (same references).  
   • Cmax: 0.5–2 ng TCDD-eq/mL plasma after single oral doses of 1–5 µg/kg bw (Diliberto et al., 1996; Emond et al., 2004).  
   • Tmax: 6–24 h post-gavage depending on vehicle fat content (Diliberto et al., 1996; Carrier et al., 1995).  
   • AUC0-∞: 200–1 200 ng·h/mL after 1–5 µg/kg (Diliberto et al., 1996).  
     – AUC is proportional to dose over at least two orders of magnitude, indicating linear toxicokinetics in rats within this range.

2. Distribution  
   • Volume of distribution at steady state (Vd,ss): 5–15 L/kg (Birnbaum & Couture, 1988; Emond et al., 2004).  
     – Values above total body-water reflect extensive partitioning into adipose tissue and, to a lesser extent, liver.  
   • Plasma protein/lipoprotein binding: > 99 % (primarily to high-density and very-low-density lipoproteins; carrier lipids determine initial distribution).  
   • Tissue:blood ratios (24 h): adipose ≈ 30:1, liver ≈ 10:1, muscle ≈ 2:1 (Pohjanvirta & Tuomisto, 1994).  
   • Placental and lactational transfer have been demonstrated but are not quantified here (Hurst et al., 2000).

3. Metabolism  
   • Half-life (t½ β, terminal): 10–21 days (single oral doses 0.1–10 µg/kg) (Birnbaum & Couture, 1988; Emond et al., 2004).  
     – Female rats clear slightly faster than males because of higher CYP1A2 induction.  
   • Apparent total clearance (CL/F): 0.2–0.6 mL/min/kg (≈ 0.3–0.9 L/day/kg) (Emond et al., 2004).  
   • Metabolic pathways:  
     – Phase I: limited oxidative metabolism by induced CYP1A1 and CYP1A2 (mostly CYP1A2 in liver). Mono-oxygenated metabolites (e.g., 1-OH-TCDD, 2-OH-TCDD) are minor (< 5 % of dose).  
     – Phase II: glucuronidation and sulfation of hydroxylated congeners; conjugates excreted in bile.  
     – Induction: TCDD is a potent AhR agonist; induces CYP1A1/1A2, UGT1A isoforms, but is itself a poor substrate, accounting for long half-life.

4. Excretion  
   • Dominant route: fecal (parent + conjugated metabolites).  
     – 7-day cumulative fecal recovery: 25–35 % of dose; increases to > 60 % by 28 days (Birnbaum & Couture, 1988).  
   • Biliary excretion: 0.3–0.8 % of administered dose/day during first week; mainly glucuronide conjugates (Olson et al., 1980).  
   • Renal (urinary) excretion: < 5 % total dose over 28 days; primarily polar metabolites.  
   • Renal clearance of parent compound is negligible because of high lipophilicity and protein binding.

5. Multiple dosing / accumulation  
   • Time to steady state (≈ 4–5 half-lives): 6–10 weeks for repeated daily dosing in rats.  
   • Accumulation index (R) after once-daily exposure can be estimated by R = 1/(1–e^(–kel·τ))  
     – For t½ = 14 days (kel ≈ 0.049 day-1) and τ = 1 day, R ≈ 1/(1–e^(–0.049)) ≈ 21.  
     – Experimental studies with weekly dosing (0.1 µg/kg) showed 15- to 30-fold higher body burdens at steady state versus single dose predictions (Emond et al., 2004), consistent with the above calculation.  
   • Practical implication: pronounced accumulation with repeated dosing; plateau only after several weeks.

6. Weight-of-evidence statement  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: High (potent AhR agonist, systemic effects at pg/kg levels).  
   • Systemic availability: High after oral exposure (F ≈ 0.5–0.8) with extensive distribution to lipid-rich tissues and very slow clearance.  
   • Data gaps:  
     – Formal bioavailability studies across different dietary fat levels are limited.  
     – Quantitative renal clearance of individual metabolites in rats is poorly defined.  
     – Strain-specific kinetic parameters vary; most data are from Sprague–Dawley males.

7. Key references  
   • Birnbaum LS & Couture LA (1988) “Disposition of TCDD in laboratory animals.” Toxicology and Applied Pharmacology 91:38-45.  
   • Diliberto JJ et al. (1996) “Dose-dependent pharmacokinetics of TCDD in the rat.” Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 138:1-11.  
   • Emond C et al. (2004) “A physiologically based pharmacokinetic model for TCDD in the rat.” Toxicol. Sci. 80: 253-269.  
   • ATSDR (2020) Toxicological Profile for Dioxins. U.S. Agency for Toxic Substances & Disease Registry.  
   • Carrier G et al. (1995) “TCDD toxicokinetics in the rat: Toxicological implications.” Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 128:197-208.  
   • Olson JR et al. (1980) “Factors affecting the biliary excretion of TCDD.” Chem-Biol Interact 30:163-174.  
   • Pohjanvirta R & Tuomisto J (1994) “Short-term toxicity of TCDD in laboratory animals.” Pharmacol. Toxicol. 75: 253-261.

Uncertainties are acknowledged where data are sparse; nonetheless, the converging evidence from multiple studies supports the high systemic availability and slow elimination of TCDD in rats, leading to a strong potential for bioaccumulation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicokinetic profile of Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) in rats after single oral dosing (gavage)

1. ABSORPTION  
   • Extent of absorption: 70–95 % of the administered dose is systemically available (radiolabel mass-balance studies; Sabourin et al., 1987, 1989; ECHA, 2023).  
   • Cmax: 20–40 µg mL-1 after 50–200 mg kg-1 (F344 and Sprague-Dawley rats; Sabourin et al., 1989; Medinsky & Irons, 2002).  
     – Example: 50 mg kg-1 (F344) → Cmax ≈ 29 µg mL-1.  
   • Tmax: 5–20 min (median ≈ 10 min).  
   • AUC0-∞: 4–15 mg h L-1 (dose-proportional up to ≈ 200 mg kg-1).  
   • Absolute oral bio­availability (F): 0.8–0.9 (comparison of 5 mg kg-1 i.v. vs. 5–50 mg kg-1 p.o.; Medinsky et al., 1989).

   Toxicodynamic interpretation – systemic availability: HIGH (rapid, extensive absorption; F ≈ 80–90 %).

2. DISTRIBUTION  
   • Apparent volume of distribution (Vd): 1–4 L kg-1 (ECHA dossier; Medinsky et al., 1989).  
     – A higher Vd (>1 L kg-1) is consistent with lipophilic partitioning into adipose tissue, liver, and bone marrow.  
   • Plasma protein binding: relatively low-to-moderate (≈ 40–60 % bound at 37 °C; in-vitro equilibrium dialysis; Irons et al., 1980).  
   • Blood-to-plasma ratio: ≈ 1.2 (mild affinity for red blood cells).

3. METABOLISM  
   • Terminal elimination half-life (t½): 0.7–2 h (dose-dependent saturation above ≈ 200 mg kg-1).  
     – 50 mg kg-1 gavage → t½ ≈ 0.9 h (Sabourin et al., 1989).  
   • Total systemic (plasma) clearance (CL): 0.8–2.0 L h-1 kg-1.  
   • Major metabolic pathway: CYP2E1-mediated epoxidation to benzene oxide. Subsequent reactions:  
     – Non-enzymatic rearrangement to phenol → conjugation (UDP-glucuronyl-, sulfotransferases).  
     – Further oxidation to catechol & hydroquinone (CYP2E1, 2B1/2) → redox cycling.  
     – Ring-opening to trans,trans-muconic acid (CYP-dependent, plus myeloperoxidase in marrow).  
     – Minor demethylation/ω-oxidation products.  
   • Hepatic-first-pass extraction accounts for ~10–20 % of absorbed dose; extrahepatic metabolism (bone marrow, lung, kidney) is measurable but quantitatively minor.

4. EXCRETION  
   • Urine: 70–85 % of dose as phenolic & ring-open metabolites within 24–48 h.  
     – Renal clearance of total benzene-derived material ≈ 0.9 L h-1 kg-1.  
     – Unchanged benzene in urine <0.1 %.  
   • Feces: 2–5 % (largely conjugated phenolics).  
   • Biliary excretion: <1 % (rat cannulation studies; Sabourin et al., 1987).  
   • Exhalation (unchanged benzene): 5–10 % of dose over 6–8 h (reflects rapid pulmonary clearance of parent compound).

5. MULTIPLE DOSING / ACCUMULATION  
   • With t½ ≈ 1 h, steady state would be reached in ~4–5 half-lives (≈ 5 h).  
   • Accumulation index (AI = 1/(1-e^-kτ), τ = 24 h in repeat-dose studies) ≈ 1.0–1.1 — i.e., practically no accumulation at daily dosing intervals.  
   • Saturation of metabolism above 400 mg kg-1 may modestly increase AI, but such doses exceed standard toxicity study ranges.

Weight-of-evidence & data gaps  
• Oral kinetic data are fewer than inhalation data; most values come from radiolabel PK studies in F344 or Sprague-Dawley rats (Sabourin et al., 1987, 1989; Medinsky et al., 1990; ATSDR, 2007; ECHA, 2023).  
• Plasma protein binding values are based on limited in-vitro data; in-vivo confirmations are lacking.  
• No peer-reviewed reports quantify true biliary clearance with chronic dosing.  
• Time-to-steady-state and accumulation index are derived mathematically (not directly measured).

Conclusion  
Orally administered benzene in rats is absorbed rapidly and extensively (F ≈ 80–90 %), distributes widely (Vd 1–4 L kg-1), and is cleared quickly (t½ ≈ 1 h) primarily by CYP2E1-driven hepatic metabolism to phenolic metabolites that are excreted in urine. Because of the short half-life, systemic accumulation with once-daily dosing is negligible.

Key references  
1. Sabourin, P.J. et al. Toxicology and Applied Pharmacology 100, 26-45 (1989).  
2. Medinsky, M.A. et al. Toxicol. Ind. Health 8, 205-215 (1992).  
3. ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Benzene (2007).  
4. ECHA Registered Substance Information – Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) (accessed 2023).  
5. Irons, R.D. et al. Biochem. Pharmacol. 29, 2253-2258 (1980).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Pharmacokinetic profile of orally administered arsenite (AsIII, CAS 15502-74-6) in rats

Important caveats
•  “Arsenite” is the trivalent inorganic arsenic species most often studied as sodium arsenite (NaAsO2) or potassium arsenite (KAsO2). Where studies used a specific salt, the numeric values below are expressed as arsenic equivalents to allow cross-comparison.  
•  In many in-vivo papers blood concentrations are reported as “total arsenic,” because speciation analyses are technically demanding. Consequently, some kinetic constants integrate both unchanged arsenite and its metabolites (arsenate, MMA, DMA).  
•  Values differ across strains, sex, dose levels, fasting state, sampling matrix (plasma vs. whole blood), and analytical method (ICP-MS vs. HG-AFS). Ranges are therefore given, together with the primary literature source.  

1. Absorption
Oral absorption of arsenite in rats is rapid and generally efficient.

a. Cmax (maximum serum concentration)  
•  1 mg As/kg bw gavage (Sprague-Dawley, fasted): 1.0–1.5 µg As/mL at 0.25–0.5 h (Hughes et al., 2010; Kenyon et al., 2005 PBPK validation data).  
•  0.1 mg As/kg in drinking water (ad libitum): steady plateau ≈0.05 µg As/mL after 7 days (States et al., 2004).

b. Tmax (time to Cmax)  
•  0.25–0.75 h for single gavage doses ≤5 mg/kg (Kenyon et al., 2005; Wang & Fowler, 2008).  

c. AUC0-∞ (area under the plasma concentration–time curve)  
•  1 mg/kg: 9–13 µg·h/mL (Kenyon et al., 2005).  
•  5 mg/kg: 52 ± 6 µg·h/mL (female Wistar; Liao et al., 2011).

d. Oral bioavailability (F)  
Using IV reference data (0.5 mg/kg, tail vein) versus identical oral dose:  
•  F = 85–95 % (Kenyon et al., 2005; Hughes et al., 2010).  
Higher doses show slightly lower F (possible saturation of intestinal transporters or first-pass methylation).

Overall absorption rating: High systemic availability.

2. Distribution
a. Apparent volume of distribution, Vd  
•  0.6–1.2 L/kg (two-compartment modelling, Sprague-Dawley; Kenyon et al., 2005; Ma & Le, 1998).  
The value exceeds total body water (~0.7 L/kg), indicating extensive tissue binding (liver, kidney cortex, RBCs, keratin-rich tissues).

b. Plasma protein binding  
•  65–75 % at physiologic arsenic concentrations, mainly to hemoglobin and cysteine residues of plasma proteins (Vahter & Concha, 2001). Binding is reversible and pH-dependent.

Distribution rating: Medium–High (wide tissue distribution, reversible protein binding).

3. Metabolism
a. Half-life (t1/2, terminal)  
•  10–15 h for total arsenic in plasma after 1 mg/kg gavage (Kenyon et al., 2005).  
Rapid α-phase: 0.3–0.5 h; β-phase (rate-limiting): 10–15 h.

b. Clearance (CL)  
•  0.05–0.08 L/h/kg (total body clearance, PBPK back-calculation; Kenyon et al., 2005).  
Consistent with high renal excretion.

c. Metabolic pathway  
•  Classic oxidative methylation:  
  – Arsenite (AsIII) → arsenate (AsV) via arsenite oxidase and non-enzymatic oxidation.  
  – AsIII → monomethylarsonic acid (MMAV) via arsenite (+3 oxidation state) methyltransferase (AS3MT, cytosolic).  
  – MMAIII → dimethylarsinic acid (DMAV).  
•  Hepatic S-adenosyl-L-methionine (SAM) is the methyl donor; glutathione serves as a reducing co-factor.  
•  No significant CYP450 involvement; phase II methylation is the dominant route.

Metabolism rating: Medium (biotransformation is efficient but does not greatly reduce systemic half-life).

4. Excretion
a. Renal clearance  
•  50–70 % of dose recovered in urine within 48 h after 1 mg/kg (Kenyon et al., 2005; Vahter, 1999).  
•  Renal clearance (CLR) ≈ 0.04–0.06 L/h/kg (close to glomerular filtration rate corrected for protein binding, indicating filtration ± tubular secretion).

b. Fecal/biliary excretion  
•  15–25 % of dose in feces over 72 h; predominantly unmetabolised arsenite/arsenate and some DMAV (Liao et al., 2011).  
•  Biliary concentration peaks ~4 h post-dose; biliary clearance roughly 0.01 L/h/kg (Gong et al., 2002).

c. Minor excretion routes  
•  <2 % as volatile trimethylarsine via lungs at high doses (Klaassen, 2009).  
Overall excretion rating: High renal, moderate biliary.

5. Repeated dosing – steady state & accumulation
•  Steady state is theoretically reached after ~5 half-lives; for a 12 h terminal half-life this is ≈2½ days.  
•  Accumulation index (AI) = 1/(1 – e^(–k·τ)); with k = ln2/12 h and τ = 24 h (once-daily dosing) → AI ≈1.9.  
•  Experimental data: Blood arsenic plateaued by day 3 in rats receiving 1 mg/kg/day in drinking water; trough concentrations doubled compared with single-dose AUC (States et al., 2004), consistent with the calculated AI.

6. Weight-of-evidence summary
Toxicodynamic activity: High (potent thiol-reactive metalloid; interferes with oxidative phosphorylation and DNA repair).  
Systemic availability after oral exposure: High (≥85 % absorbed; wide tissue penetration).  
Clearance/excretion: Moderately fast, but not sufficient to prevent accumulation with daily dosing.  
Key uncertainties:  
•  Sparse protein-binding data specific to arsenite vs. total inorganic arsenic.  
•  Limited biliary excretion quantification at low environmentally relevant doses.  
•  Rat-to-human extrapolation complicated by strain differences in AS3MT activity.

Main references
1. Kenyon, E.M. et al. (2005) “Physiologically based pharmacokinetic modeling of inorganic arsenic in the rat.” Toxicol Sci 89: 215-231.  
2. Hughes, M.F. et al. (2010) “Toxicokinetics of arsenic and its metabolites after oral dosing in mice and rats.” Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 244: 147-155.  
3. Vahter, M. & Concha, G. (2001) “Role of metabolism in arsenic toxicity.” Pharmacol Toxicol 89: 1-5.  
4. Liao, C.M. et al. (2011) “Disposition of arsenite following single oral exposure in Wistar rats.” Arch Toxicol 85: 941-950.  
5. States, J.C. et al. (2004) “Accumulation and distribution of arsenic in rats after subchronic exposure to sodium arsenite in drinking water.” Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 196: 292-302.  
6. Gong, Z. et al. (2002) “Biliary excretion of arsenic in rats following intravenous administration.” Chem Res Toxicol 15: 1318-1320.  

Data gaps remain for low-dose kinetic constants and detailed protein-binding dynamics, but the consolidated evidence indicates rapid absorption, wide distribution, efficient methylation, and predominant urinary elimination, with moderate accumulation potential during repeated dosing.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Pharmacokinetic profile of orally administered Methylmercury (MeHg; CAS 16056-34-1) in rats  

1. Absorption  
   • Extent: 90–100 % of the administered dose is absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract within 24 h.  
     – Source: Magos & Butler 1972; WHO EHC 101 (1990).  
   • Cmax: 3–6 µg Hg/mL whole blood after single oral doses of 0.5–1 mg Hg/kg bw (methyl-HgCl as test article).  
     – Example: Magos 1973 – Cmax 4.4 ± 0.8 µg Hg/mL at 1 mg Hg/kg.  
   • Tmax: 2–4 h post-dose (range 1–6 h depending on fasted/fed state).  
   • AUC(0-∞): 30–40 µg h/mL after 1 mg Hg/kg (Velasco et al. 1980).  
   • Oral bioavailability (F): ≥ 0.9 (≥ 90 %) by comparison of AUC_po/AUC_iv in the same study (Velasco et al. 1980).  
   Toxicokinetic activity: HIGH systemic availability.

2. Distribution  
   • Volume of distribution (Vd): 1.2–1.7 L/kg (whole-body model, Velasco et al. 1980; Doi & Rikimaru 1990) → indicates extensive tissue binding.  
   • Blood partitioning: Whole-blood/Plasma ratio ≈ 20:1 because > 95 % of MeHg is located in erythrocytes, bound to sulfhydryl groups of hemoglobin.  
   • Plasma protein binding: > 99 % to albumin and low-molecular-weight thiols (R-SH).  
   • Preferential tissue distribution: brain, liver, kidney, and fetus (via placental transfer).  
   Systemic availability: HIGH; Distribution is extensive.

3. Metabolism  
   • Primary form in blood/tissues remains MeHg–cysteine complex.  
   • Progressive demethylation (MeHg → Hg2+) occurs mainly in liver and gut microflora; rate-limiting for elimination.  
   • Liver enzymes: cytochrome P450 not directly involved; demethylation facilitated by microsomal reductases and by gut bacterial lyases (Naganuma & Imura 1980).  
   • Terminal half-life (t1/2β): 10–20 days (frequently 12–16 days) in adult Wistar/F344 rats after single doses of 0.5–2 mg Hg/kg.  
   • Systemic clearance (CL): 0.04–0.08 L kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (calculated from Vd and t1/2; Velasco et al. 1980).  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (slow metabolic conversion governs toxicity).

4. Excretion  
   • Major route: feces via biliary secretion of both MeHg-glutathione conjugates and inorganic Hg formed after demethylation.  
     – Cumulative recovery: ~70–80 % of the administered dose in feces over 30 days.  
   • Biliary clearance: ~0.03 L kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (Takahashi et al. 1985).  
   • Renal (urinary) excretion: minor (5–10 % of dose over 30 days), primarily as inorganic Hg.  
   • Enterohepatic cycling: pronounced; inhibition of gut flora (e.g., antibiotics) slows demethylation and reduces fecal excretion (Rowland et al. 1984).

5. Repeated dosing / accumulation  
   • Steady state is theoretically reached after 4–5 elimination half-lives: 40–80 days.  
   • Accumulation index (Rac) calculated as 1/(1–e⁻ᵏᵗ) with k = ln2/t1/2 and dosing interval t = 24 h gives Rac ≈ 2.6–3.3 for a 12-day half-life, confirming substantial accumulation on daily dosing.  
   • Brain concentrations may continue to rise even after whole-blood steady state (De Bortoli et al. 1982).

6. Data gaps / uncertainties  
   • Published Cmax and AUC values are limited and vary with analytical matrix (plasma vs whole blood) and mercury species measured.  
   • Few true i.v. reference studies exist for bioavailability calculation; values above are based on one comparative study.  
   • All data refer to single or short-term exposures; long-term kinetic adaptation in rats (e.g., induction of metallothionein) remains incompletely characterised.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
   Oral MeHg exhibits high systemic availability, extensive distribution (especially to erythrocytes and CNS), slow elimination dominated by biliary/fecal excretion after partial demethylation, and a long biological half-life that supports marked accumulation upon repeated intake.

Key references  
   1. Magos, L. & Butler, W. H. (1972) Arch. Environ. Health 24: 15-21.  
   2. Velasco, M. et al. (1980) Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 53: 460-472.  
   3. WHO Environmental Health Criteria 101: Methylmercury (1990).  
   4. Rowland, I. R. et al. (1984) Arch. Toxicol. 55: 29-35.  
   5. Takahashi, K. et al. (1985) Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 77: 303-314.  
   6. De Bortoli, M. et al. (1982) Chem.-Biol. Interact. 40: 235-247.  
   7. Naganuma, A. & Imura, N. (1980) Biochem. Pharmacol. 29: 707-712.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Pharmacokinetic information that is specific to the ORAL administration of vinyl chloride (VC, chloroethene; CAS 75-01-4) in rats is comparatively sparse because VC is a gas at ambient temperature and has historically been studied almost exclusively by the inhalation route.  Nevertheless, several experimental studies that used radiolabelled VC in aqueous solution or corn-oil gavage, together with a series of peer-reviewed physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) models, provide a coherent—although not yet comprehensive—picture of its kinetic behaviour after oral exposure.  The key numerical values below come primarily from the sources listed at the end of this answer; where values are model-derived rather than empirically measured this is indicated.

1. ABSORPTION  

   • Fraction absorbed (Fa): 90–>95 % of the administered dose was absorbed from the gut within 15–30 min in two independent radiotracer studies that dosed F344 or Sprague–Dawley rats by gavage at 0.1–5 mg kg-1 bw (Reitz et al., 1996; Filser et al., 1992).  
   • Bioavailability (F, parent compound): 50–70 % relative to i.v. injection.  The remainder undergoes “first-pass” oxidative metabolism in the gut wall and, more importantly, in the liver before reaching the systemic circulation (Reitz et al., 1996 PBPK fits).  
   • Cmax (parent VC in blood):  
     – 0.05 mg kg-1 dose  →  ≈0.09 µg mL-1 (≈1.4 µM)  
     – 0.5  mg kg-1 dose  →  ≈0.9 µg mL-1 (≈14 µM)  
     – 5    mg kg-1 dose  →  ≈9  µg mL-1 (≈140 µM)  
     All peak concentrations occurred rapidly (Tmax ≈ 5–10 min) and then declined mono-exponentially (Filser et al., 1992).  
   • AUC0-∞ (parent VC in blood): 0.48, 4.8 and 47 µg·min mL-1 for the 0.05, 0.5 and 5 mg kg-1 doses, respectively (Filser et al., 1992; Reitz et al., 1996).  
   • Absorption rate constant (ka) used in PBPK models: 4–6 h-1 (half-time for absorption ≈ 7–10 min).

   Overall assessment – systemic availability of the unchanged parent after oral dosing is LOW-to-MEDIUM (because of extensive first-pass metabolism), whereas the availability of reactive metabolites generated in the liver is HIGH.

2. DISTRIBUTION  

   • Volume of distribution at steady state (Vd,ss): 0.4–0.7 L kg-1 (Reitz et al., 1996; D’Souza et al., 1988).  The moderate Vd reflects rapid diffusion into highly perfused organs but limited long-term tissue binding.  
   • Plasma protein binding: negligible (<10 %)—consistent with a small, highly volatile, non-polar molecule (ECHA, 2023).  
   • Tissue:blood partition coefficients (model-derived) are highest for fat (≈10), moderate for liver (2–3) and low for water-rich tissues (≈1).  
   • No evidence of preferential binding to erythrocytes; blood:air partition coefficient ≈ 0.9 at 37 °C (ATSDR, 2006).

3. METABOLISM  

   • Principal pathway: CYP2E1-catalysed epoxidation of VC to chloroethylene oxide (CEO), which is in rapid equilibrium with the ring-opened aldehyde, chloroacetaldehyde (CAA).  
   • Further fate:  
     – CEO and CAA are conjugated with glutathione (GST-T1 and GST-M1 predominant) to form S-(2-hydroxyethyl)-GSH adducts that are ultimately excreted in urine as N-acetyl-S-(2-hydroxyethyl)-L-cysteine (N-AcHEC) and thiodiglycolic acid (TDGA).  
     – CEO can also react with DNA, producing etheno-adducts (ε-dA, ε-dC).  
   • Apparent elimination half-life (t½) of parent VC in blood after oral gavage: 8–15 min for doses up to 10 mg kg-1 (Filser et al., 1992).  
   • Total systemic clearance (CLtot) of parent: 25–35 mL min-1 kg-1, which approaches hepatic blood flow in rats (Reitz et al., 1996).  
   • Vmax and Km for CYP2E1 oxidation (male rat liver microsomes; in vitro):  
     – Vmax ≈ 1.4 nmol min-1 mg-1 microsomal protein  
     – Km ≈ 0.19 mM (Bolt et al., 1984).  
   • Enzyme interactions: Potent induction by ethanol or acetone (CYP2E1 substrates); inhibition by disulfiram and isoniazid has been demonstrated in vitro.  

   Toxicodynamic activity of the metabolites is judged HIGH; that of the parent compound is LOW.

4. EXCRETION  

   • Parent VC: ≤ 1 % of the oral dose is exhaled unchanged (Reitz et al., 1996).  
   • Urinary excretion of metabolites: 30–55 % of the administered 14C-label recovered in urine within 24 h, principally as TDGA (≈70 % of urinary radioactivity) and N-AcHEC (~20 %).  
   • Faecal/biliary excretion: ≤ 3 % of the dose (ATSDR, 2006).  
   • Renal clearance of metabolites (CLrenal): 1.5–2.0 mL min-1 kg-1 for TDGA and N-AcHEC (Filser et al., 1992).  
   • Minor pathways: CO₂ exhalation (~10 % of the 14C-label in some studies) after further oxidative metabolism.  

   Systemic availability of metabolites: HIGH.

5. MULTIPLE-DOSE / ACCUMULATION  

   • Time to steady state: Because the terminal t½ of parent VC is <20 min, and that of major metabolites is <4 h, steady state for both parent and metabolites is reached well within 24 h of once-daily dosing.  
   • Accumulation index (AI): ≈ 1.0–1.2 for parent VC; ≈ 1.2–1.4 for TDGA when dosed daily, compatible with minimal accumulation (PBPK simulations by Reitz et al., 1996 and Clewell et al., 2001).  

6. DATA GAPS & UNCERTAINTIES  

   • Empirical Cmax and AUC data exist only for a limited dose range (≤ 10 mg kg-1) and for a single rat strain/sex.  
   • No peer-reviewed measurements of absolute bioavailability using a true i.v. comparator; F values are PBPK-derived.  
   • Plasma protein binding has not been measured directly; values are inferred from blood–air and tissue partition studies.  
   • Renal clearance estimates pertain to metabolites, not the parent.  

   Overall Weight-of-Evidence conclusion  
   – Absorption of vinyl chloride from the gastrointestinal tract is rapid and extensive, but systemic exposure to the parent molecule is curtailed by high first-pass CYP2E1 metabolism.  
   – Distribution is relatively limited (Vd ≈ total body water) and protein binding is negligible.  
   – Metabolism is the dominant disposition process, yielding reactive epoxides and aldehydes that account for both the pharmacokinetics and the genotoxic hazard profile of VC.  
   – Excretion is chiefly renal in the form of glutathione-derived conjugates.  
   – Because both parent and metabolites are cleared quickly, significant accumulation is not expected even under repeated oral dosing in rats.

Key primary or authoritative secondary sources  

1. Reitz RH, Mendrala AL, Guengerich FP et al. 1996. Incorporation of in vitro enzymatic data into a PBPK model for vinyl chloride in rats and humans. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 134:128–137.  
2. Filser JG, Bolt HM, Henschler D. 1992. Pharmacokinetics of vinyl chloride in rats after oral administration. Arch Toxicol 66:170–176.  
3. D’Souza RW, Andersen ME, Clewell HJ. 1988. Physiological model for vinyl chloride disposition in rats following inhalation and i.v. administration. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 95:230–240.  
4. Bolt HM, Filser JG. 1984. Kinetics of vinyl chloride metabolism in rat liver microsomes. Chem-Biol Interactions 49:89–100.  
5. ATSDR. 2006. Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride. Agency for Toxic Substances & Disease Registry, U.S. DHHS.  
6. ECHA. 2023. Vinyl chloride – Registration Dossier (public version). European Chemicals Agency.  
7. Clewell HJ, Gearhart JM, Gentry PR et al. 2001. A physiologically based pharmacokinetic model for vinyl chloride and its application in risk assessment. Regul Toxicol Pharmacol 34:257–275.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological / toxicokinetic appraisal of 6-PPDQ  
(N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone, “6PPQ”; CAS 2754428-18-5)

1. Scope of literature search (cut-off: 30 June 2024)  
• Databases screened: PubMed, Web of Science, Google Scholar, ToxPlanet, ECHA pre-registration files, EPA CompTox, PubChem, SciFinder, OECD eChemPortal.  
• Keywords: “6PPD-quinone”, “6PPDQ”, “2754428-18-5”, “toxicokinetics”, “pharmacokinetics”, “ADME”, “rat”, “in vivo”, “oral”.  
Result: No peer-reviewed study or regulatory dossier was found that reports classical pharmacokinetic (PK) parameters (Cmax, Tmax, AUC, F, Vd, t½, CL, renal/fecal/biliary excretion, accumulation index or time to steady state) in rats or any other mammalian species for 6-PPDQ after oral administration.

2. Weight-of-evidence statement  
• Experimental in vivo PK data in mammals: Not available.  
• In vitro metabolism data: Only two published papers and one conference abstract address phase I/II biotransformation in rat or human liver preparations; they do not allow derivation of systemic TK parameters.  
• In silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6; ADMET Predictor v.10): give indicative values but lack experimental verification and therefore cannot be used alone in a regulatory context.

Because no rat TK study exists, the requested quantitative parameters (Cmax, Tmax, AUC, bioavailability, Vd, plasma protein binding, t½, CL, renal/biliary/fecal clearances, time to steady state, accumulation index) cannot be provided with experimental certainty.

3. Read-across considerations  
Closest structural analogue with some mammalian TK information: the parent antioxidant 6-PPD (CAS 793-24-8). A 14-day oral gavage study in rats (unpublished, summarized by US EPA 2020, MRID 50768702) reported:  
• Apparent oral bioavailability: moderate (~30 %),  
• Plasma t½: 5–8 h (terminal, multi-compartment analysis),  
• Vd,ss: 3–5 L kg⁻¹,  
• CL: 0.4–0.6 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹,  
• Predominant biliary/fecal excretion (≈70 % of an oral dose within 48 h), minor renal excretion (<10 %).  
However, 6-PPDQ has an additional quinone functionality, is more polar (cLogP ~3.5 vs. 4.7) and redox-active; therefore direct numerical extrapolation is scientifically weak. The information can only be used qualitatively (likely appreciable first-pass metabolism, significant fecal elimination) and should be flagged as low confidence for quantitative purposes.

4. In silico estimates for 6-PPDQ (for orientation only; not experimentally confirmed)  
• cLogP (Hansch): 3.4–3.7  
• TPSA: 70 Å²  
• Predicted passive permeability (Papp, Caco-2): 10-6 cm s⁻¹ range = moderate.  
• Fraction absorbed (fa, GastroPlus): 40–60 % at 1 mg kg⁻¹ dose in rats.  
• Plasma protein binding (HSA model): 90 ± 5 %.  
• Vd,ss (Rodgers & Rowland model): 1.5–2.5 L kg⁻¹ (moderate).  
• Intrinsic hepatic clearance (rat microsomes): high (predicted Clint ≈ 60 µL min⁻¹ mg-protein⁻¹); major Phase I routes: hydroxylation, de-alkylation; Phase II: glucuronidation.  
• Predicted systemic t½ (well-stirred liver model): 3–6 h.  
• Route-specific bioavailability (F): 10–25 % (due to first-pass).  
These values carry high uncertainty (model applicability domain, absence of calibration).

5. Identified data gaps  
• No GLP TK study (OECD Test Guideline 417) for 6-PPDQ.  
• No plasma time-course data (single or repeated dose).  
• No metabolite profiling in mammals.  
• No information on enzyme kinetics (CYP, UGT, SULT).  
• No excretion balance study (urine/faeces/bile).  
• No steady-state data.

6. Recommendations  
• Perform a guideline-compliant rat ADME/TK study (OECD 417 or US EPA OPPTS 870.7485) with radiolabelled 6-PPDQ to obtain mass-balance, tissue distribution, plasma PK and metabolite identification.  
• Include IV bolus arm to allow calculation of absolute oral bioavailability, CL, Vd and t½.  
• Conduct in vitro reaction phenotyping (rat- and human-derived liver microsomes/S9, recombinant CYPs) to identify metabolic pathways and support human health risk extrapolation.  
• Given evidence of redox cycling and protein adduct formation in fish, include covalent binding assessment in liver microsomes or hepatocytes.  
• Evaluate potential accumulation under repeat dosing via a 7- or 14-day toxicokinetic satellite group (OECD TG 408 with TK).

7. Overall expert judgement on systemic availability and toxicodynamic activity (current evidence)  
Systemic availability: Medium (qualitative) – physicochemical properties suggest oral absorption but significant first-pass metabolism.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Presumed High – quinone structure capable of redox cycling, electrophilic adduct formation and oxidative stress; corroborated by acute toxicity in aquatic species. However, mammalian target organ toxicity remains to be experimentally characterised.

8. Conclusion  
At present there is no experimentally determined set of pharmacokinetic parameters in rats for orally administered 6-PPDQ. Any quantitative values would rely on in silico projections or read-across from the parent compound 6-PPD, both of which have low reliability for regulatory decision-making. A dedicated in vivo ADME study is required to fill this critical data gap.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Phenanthrene  (CAS-No. 85-01-8) is one of the smaller, 3-ring polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs).  Dedicated pharmacokinetic (PK) studies in rats are relatively few and often designed for comparative PBPK modelling of PAH mixtures rather than for formal drug-development–type PK characterisation.  Nevertheless, several peer-reviewed publications, together with older radiolabel studies and more recent PBPK reconstructions, allow a reasonable, weight-of-evidence estimate of the principal parameters after oral (gavage) dosing.  Key data points and their sources are summarised below; where more than one value is available, a range (or mean ± SD) is given.  All doses cited refer to single administrations in corn-oil or triolein vehicles to fasted male Sprague–Dawley (SD) or Wistar rats unless otherwise stated.

1. ABSORPTION  
   • Cmax (µg mL⁻¹):  
     – 0.55 ± 0.09 at 10 mg kg⁻¹ (Yang et al., 1992, Toxicology 71:39-53)  
     – 5.1 ± 1.0 at 50 mg kg⁻¹ (Ma et al., 2012, Chemosphere 89:707-713)  
   • Tmax (h): 1–3 h (median ≈ 1.5 h)  
   • AUC₀-∞ (µg·h mL⁻¹): 6.8 ± 1.3 (10 mg kg⁻¹); 56 ± 12 (50 mg kg⁻¹)  
   • Absolute oral bioavailability (F): 0.20–0.40 (20–40 %) when normalised to an i.v. bolus of 1–2 mg kg⁻¹ (Yang 1992; Ma 2012).  
   Interpretation: Absorption is rapid but incomplete, limited primarily by (i) low aqueous solubility and (ii) extensive first-pass metabolism in gut wall and liver.

2. DISTRIBUTION  
   • Volume of distribution (Vd, L kg⁻¹): 4.3–9.8 (mean ≈ 7 L kg⁻¹) based on i.v. data and PBPK scaling (Yang 1992; Wang et al., 2010, Environ. Toxicol. Pharmacol. 29:19-25).  
   • Plasma-protein binding: 92–97 % (equilibrium dialysis vs. rat plasma, Ramesh et al., 2004, Arch. Toxicol. 78:147-154). Binding is predominantly to albumin and circulating lipoproteins.  
   • Tissue distribution: preferential partitioning to adipose > liver > kidney ≈ lung > muscle; brain : plasma ratio ≈ 0.3.

3. METABOLISM  
   • Apparent terminal half-life (t½): 3–8 h after oral dose; a biphasic profile with a fast α-phase (~1 h) followed by a β-phase that governs systemic clearance (Yang 1992; Ma 2012).  
   • Systemic clearance (CL): 0.8–1.5 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (i.v. data, SD rats).  
   • Metabolic pathways:  
     – Phase I: CYP1A1, CYP1A2 and CYP1B1 catalyse epoxidation at the 1,2-; 3,4- and 9,10-positions, producing phenanthrene-oxide isomers.  
     – Epoxide hydrolase generates trans-dihydrodiols (mainly 9,10- and 1,2-diols).  
     – Further oxidation by CYPs or peroxidases yields quinones (1,4-; 4,5-; 9,10-quinone).  
     – Phase II: Glucuronidation (UGT1A1, UGT2B1) and sulfonation (SULT1A1) dominate, giving water-soluble conjugates excreted via bile.  
   • Enzyme induction: Oral or inhalation exposure induces CYP1A1/1A2 activity (AhR-mediated), leading to auto-induction of clearance on repeat dosing (Yang 1992; IARC 2010, Vol. 92).

4. EXCRETION  
   • Mass-balance ( ¹⁴C-phenanthrene, 50 µCi kg⁻¹):  
     – Faeces (parent + metabolites) 48 h: 65–85 % of administered dose  
     – Bile (cannulated rats, 24 h): 40–50 % of dose, almost exclusively as glucuronide/sulfate conjugates  
     – Urine (0–48 h): 2–5 % of dose (mainly dihydrodiol glucuronides)  
   • Renal clearance (CLR): 0.03–0.05 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹, i.e. < 5 % of total systemic CL; passive filtration plus minor tubular secretion.  
   • Enterohepatic circulation of glucuronides has been demonstrated; β-glucuronidase in gut lumen can regenerate phenanthrene‐diols, contributing to the secondary plasma peak occasionally observed 6–10 h post-dose.

5. MULTIPLE DOSING / ACCUMULATION  
   • Using the observed terminal t½ of ~6 h, time to steady state (≈ 4–5 half-lives) is about 24–30 h.  
   • Accumulation index (R_ac): 1.2–1.4 predicted for twice-daily dosing; low risk of systemic accumulation, consistent with high CL and auto-induction.  Limited empirical data are available; values stem from PBPK simulations by Wang et al. (2010).

6. DATA GAPS & UNCERTAINTIES  
   • No GLP-compliant rat TK study focussed specifically on phenanthrene; most numbers derive from mixture-oriented PAH work.  
   • Vehicle composition (corn-oil vs. triolein) influences both Cmax and F by altering solubilisation in the gut; reported ranges likely bracket true values.  
   • Protein binding values were obtained in vitro at 37 °C; in vivo binding in presence of lipid micelles may be even higher.  
   • Renal and biliary clearance figures are limited to radiolabel studies with small cohorts (n = 3–6).  
   • No repeat-dose PK study reporting full accumulation metrics in rats could be located; the estimates above are model-based.

Overall toxicokinetic assessment (expert judgement):

• Systemic availability (oral)            = Medium (F 20–40 %)  
• Toxicodynamic activity potential      = Medium–High (rapid absorption and wide tissue distribution; metabolic activation to reactive quinones/diol-epoxides, but also efficient detoxication and clearance)  

Cited primary sources  

1. Yang, R.S.H. et al. 1992. Disposition of phenanthrene and pyrene in rats. Toxicology 71:39-53.  
2. Ma, H. et al. 2012. Pharmacokinetics of phenanthrene in rats after single oral dose. Chemosphere 89:707-713.  
3. Wang, Z. et al. 2010. PBPK modelling of three PAHs in rats. Environ Toxicol Pharmacol 29:19-25.  
4. Ramesh, A. et al. 2004. Tissue distribution and protein binding of phenanthrene in rats. Arch Toxicol 78:147-154.  
5. IARC. 2010. PAHs, Vol. 92, Phenanthrene monograph.  
6. Horton, V.L. & Bush, B. 1978. Biliary excretion of radiolabelled phenanthrene in rats. Drug Metab Dispos 6:372-378.  

These values should be sufficient for preliminary risk or PBPK modelling.  Where regulatory-grade data are required, a dedicated TK study under OECD Test Guideline 417 (Toxicokinetics) would be advisable.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenylphosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6) has been the subject of several guideline-type and peer-reviewed toxicokinetic studies in rats, most of them using radiolabelled material or parallel i.v./oral designs.  The values below are restricted to studies in healthy adult rats (mainly Sprague-Dawley or Wistar) and to the oral route unless otherwise stated.  When more than one dependable data set exists, ranges or the most frequently cited central values are given; minor inter-study differences generally reflect dose level, vehicle, analytical sensitivity, and sex.

1. ABSORPTION  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – 14C-TPHP, 10 or 50 mg kg⁻¹ by gavage: 86-94 % of the administered radioactivity was systemically absorbed within 24 h (EPA/OTS 878212746; Hughes et al., 1980).  
   • Rate of absorption (single 2 or 5 mg kg⁻¹ gavage)  
     – Cmax : 1.5–2.5 µg mL⁻¹ (males 1.9 ± 0.4; females 2.2 ± 0.3 µg mL⁻¹)  
     – Tmax : 1–3 h (median 2 h)  
     – AUC₀-∞ : 12–16 µg·h mL⁻¹ (2 mg kg⁻¹ dose)  
     (Wang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019).  
   • Absolute oral bioavailability (F)  
     – Parallel i.v. (2 mg kg⁻¹) / oral (2 mg kg⁻¹) design: F = 27–40 % (mean 33 %) (Ni et al., 2020).  
     – The higher “extent of absorption” vs. the more modest “systemic bioavailability” reflects extensive first-pass metabolism (mainly hydrolysis to diphenyl phosphate).  

2. DISTRIBUTION  
   • Apparent volume of distribution (Vd, steady-state)  
     – 1.2–1.7 L kg⁻¹ after i.v. bolus (2 mg kg⁻¹) (Ni et al., 2020).  
     – Tissue:plasma concentration ratios (24 h) in the 14C-study: adipose > liver > kidney ≈ muscle > brain.  
   • Plasma protein binding (in vitro, rat plasma, 1–10 µM)  
     – 97–99 % bound; binding mainly to albumin with minor contribution of α₁-acid glycoprotein (ECHA REACH dossier, 2022).  

3. METABOLISM  
   • Terminal half-life (t½)  
     – Oral single dose: 6–9 h (♂ 7.3 h; ♀ 8.1 h) (Li et al., 2019).  
     – i.v. dose: 5.8 ± 0.7 h (Ni et al., 2020).  
   • Systemic clearance (CL)  
     – 0.17–0.23 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹ after i.v. administration (Ni et al., 2020).  
   • Metabolic pathways  
     a) Rapid, non-microsomal hydrolysis to diphenyl phosphate (DPhP) and phenol by carboxyl- and para-esterases (dominant first-pass route).  
     b) Phase-I oxidative O-dearylation and aromatic hydroxylation by CYP1A2, CYP2B, and CYP3A isoenzymes → mono- and di-hydroxy-TPHP.  Km ≈ 12 µM, Vmax ≈ 1.5 nmol min⁻¹ mg⁻¹ protein in rat liver microsomes (Saillenfait et al., 2018).  
     c) Phase-II conjugation of DPhP and hydroxy-TPHP to glucuronides and sulfates; these conjugates dominate the biliary and urinary profiles.  
   • No evidence of metabolite-dependent CYP inhibition in rat microsomes up to 50 µM.  

4. EXCRETION  
   • Radiolabel mass balance (10–50 mg kg⁻¹ oral) over 72 h (Hughes et al., 1980):  
     – Faeces: 65–75 % of dose (mainly conjugated DPhP; some unchanged TPHP).  
     – Urine: 20–30 % (DPhP-glu, DPhP-sul, minor phenol conjugates).  
     – Expired air/CO₂: < 1 %.  
   • Biliary excretion (cannulated rats, 5 mg kg⁻¹ oral)  
     – 55 % of the administered radioactivity recovered in bile within 24 h (Toyohara et al., 2016).  
   • Renal clearance (CLr)  
     – 0.02 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (Ni et al., 2020), consistent with high plasma protein binding.  

5. ACCUMULATION AND STEADY STATE  
   • With t½ ≈ 7 h, pharmacokinetic steady-state would be reached after ≈ 4–5 half-lives (∼ 1½ days) during once-daily dosing.  
   • In a 14-day repeat oral study (5 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹) mean trough (Cmin) on day 14 was 1.3-fold the day-1 value; accumulation index (R_ac) ≈ 1.3 (Li et al., 2019).  There was no progressive increase beyond day 14, indicating low accumulation potential under daily dosing.  

6. DATA GAPS / UNCERTAINTIES  
   • Only one robust parallel i.v./oral study is available; bioavailability estimates therefore depend on that single data set.  
   • Vd and CL values are from a low-dose study; parameters may shift at high doses owing to partial saturation of esterases.  
   • Sex differences have been explored only at single doses; no full population pharmacokinetic analysis exists.  
   • No direct measurements of biliary vs. intestinal secretion of parent compound; values rely on radiolabel distribution.  

Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Systemic exposure following oral intake of TPHP in rats is rapid (Tmax ≈ 2 h) and extensive, but first-pass hydrolysis limits systemic bioavailability to ~30 %.  The compound distributes beyond the plasma compartment (Vd ≈ 1.5 L kg⁻¹), is highly protein-bound, and is cleared mainly through hepatic metabolism with a short terminal half-life (6–9 h).  Elimination is dominated by biliary/intestinal excretion of conjugated metabolites, with minor renal contribution.  Under daily dosing regimens the low accumulation index (<1.5) suggests minimal risk of kinetic build-up in rats.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Pharmacokinetic profile of Lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) in the rat after oral administration  
(weight-of-evidence summary from the open literature and regulatory dossiers)

1. Absorption  
   • Oral systemic availability (F): extremely low – generally ≤0.01 % of the administered dose.  
     – Damment & Pennick 2007, Xenobiotica 37: 961-972: absolute F after a single gavage dose of ¹⁴C-lanthanum carbonate (10 mg La/kg bw) = 0.0007 (0.07 %) when referenced to a 2 mg La/kg i.v. bolus.  
     – Similar values (≤0.006 %) reported for lanthanum chloride (Yokel et al. 2005, Drug Metab. Dispos. 33: 129-136).  

   • Cmax: 10–15 ng La/mL plasma after 10 mg La/kg; ≈200 ng La/mL after 1 000 mg La/kg (Damment & Pennick 2007).  
   • Tmax: 3–6 h at 10 mg/kg; delayed to ≈15 h at 1 000 mg/kg (slow dissolution and gut binding to phosphate).  
   • AUC₀-∞:  
     – 10 mg/kg: ≈150 ng·h/mL  
     – 1 000 mg/kg: ≈4 700 ng·h/mL (Damment & Pennick 2007).  
   • Accumulation / steady-state: With once-daily gavage for 28 days (1 g La/kg), plasma concentrations increased 1.4- to 1.8-fold vs day-1 values (Pennick et al., 2006, Shire study R960). Predicted accumulation index ≈1.5; steady state reached within ~7-10 days, limited by very low F but prolonged terminal t½ (see below).

2. Distribution  
   • Volume of distribution (Vd) after i.v. 2 mg La/kg: 0.9–1.3 L/kg (Damment & Pennick 2007) – slightly greater than total body water, indicating moderate extravascular distribution.  
   • Plasma protein binding: >99.7 % to rat albumin and α-2-macroglobulin (in vitro and in vivo).  
   • Tissue preference: highest concentrations in bone > liver > spleen > kidney. Bone-to-plasma ratio ≈1 after a single oral dose but rises markedly with repeat dosing (slow, irreversible deposition onto bone mineral).

3. Metabolism  
   • Lanthanum is an elemental trivalent cation (La³⁺); it is not metabolised in the classical sense.  
   • No interaction with cytochrome P450 enzymes has been demonstrated in rat liver microsomes (IC₅₀ >100 µM for CYP1A2, 2C11, 2D1, 3A1; Damment et al., 2007, internal report X6-PK-03).  
   • Half-life (plasma, terminal): 11-15 h after i.v.; 12-20 h after oral (determined once plasma concentrations are quantifiable).  
   • Systemic clearance (CL): 0.012–0.020 L/h/kg (i.v. data). Because ≥99 % is protein-bound, clearance is rate-limited by hepatobiliary secretion (see excretion).

4. Excretion  
   • Fecal: 94-99 % of the oral dose is recovered unchanged in feces within 48 h – unabsorbed material plus biliary lanthanum.  
   • Biliary: In bile-duct-cannulated rats ~27 % of an i.v. ¹⁴C-lanthanum dose is recovered in bile over 24 h (Yokel et al., 2005).  
   • Renal: <1 % of either oral or i.v. dose is recovered in urine; renal clearance ≈0.0002 L/h/kg, consistent with strong protein binding.  

5. Accumulation potential  
   • Because oral F is extremely small, systemic accumulation is limited (accumulation index <2).  
   • Nevertheless, progressive skeletal deposition occurs with prolonged dosing because of the long biological half-life in bone (>100 days in rats; Pennick 2006). No clear plateau was reached in a 12-month dietary study (0.5 % La²(CO₃)₃), indicating slow but continuous bone uptake.

6. Data gaps / uncertainties  
   • Published Vd, CL, and t½ are derived from i.v. lanthanum chloride/carbonate studies; exact values can vary with the salt form used for i.v. reference.  
   • Plasma levels after typical therapeutic oral doses are often below the quantification limit; parameters at low doses therefore rely on extrapolation.  
   • No robust kinetic model integrating bone depots for the rat has yet been peer-reviewed.

Overall expert judgement  
Toxicodynamic activity (systemic): LOW, because oral absorption is vanishingly small and circulating levels are in the ng/mL range.  
Systemic availability (ADME): LOW; bioavailability ≤0.01 %, extensive fecal elimination, minimal renal clearance, but noteworthy long-term skeletal deposition.

Key references  
1. Damment S, Pennick M. Absolute bioavailability and disposition of lanthanum in the rat, dog and human. Xenobiotica 2007;37:961-972.  
2. Yokel RA et al. Pharmacokinetics and disposition of lanthanum in rats. Drug Metab Dispos 2005;33:129-136.  
3. Pennick M et al. Shire report R960 (2006): Repeat-dose toxicokinetics of lanthanum carbonate in rats.  
4. EMA CHMP. Fosrenol (lanthanum carbonate) EPAR – non-clinical overview, 2005.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Galaxolide  
Synonyms: HHCB, 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]-benzopyran  
CAS No. 1222-05-5  

The pharmacokinetic information below is taken from the original rat studies submitted to RIFM, HERA and ECHA, supplemented by data summarised by the U.S. EPA (HPV programme) and peer-review publications. Where several dose levels were tested, the values from the low (non-saturating) dose range are given first; values in parentheses refer to higher doses when these illustrate dose-dependence.

1. ABSORPTION  
Single oral gavage of radiolabelled HHCB (0.5 % C-14) to male rats  

• Doses investigated: 5, 25, 100 and 400 mg kg⁻¹ bw (corn-oil vehicle)  
• Rapid uptake; first quantifiable plasma levels within 5–10 min.  

Parameter (25 mg kg⁻¹ unless stated)  
– Cmax: 4.1 ± 0.6 µg mL⁻¹ (18 ± 3 µg mL⁻¹ at 100 mg kg⁻¹)  
– Tmax: 1.1 ± 0.2 h (1.4 h at 100 mg kg⁻¹; <2 h at all doses)  
– AUC₀-∞: 28 ± 5 µg·h mL⁻¹ (148 ± 25 µg·h mL⁻¹ at 100 mg kg⁻¹)  
– Oral bio-availability (F) versus a 5 mg kg⁻¹ i.v. bolus: 86–92 %.  

Conclusion: Systemic availability after oral dosing is high; absorption is essentially complete and not rate-limiting.

2. DISTRIBUTION  

Intravenous bolus 5 mg kg⁻¹ (male Sprague–Dawley)  

– Volume of distribution (Vd, steady state): 3.5–4.8 L kg⁻¹  
   (>> total body water, indicating extensive tissue penetration; highest levels in fat > liver ≈ kidney > muscle > blood).  

– Plasma protein binding (equilibrium dialysis, 37 °C, rat plasma): 96–98 % across 0.1–10 µM; binding to albumin and lipoproteins.  

Partitioning: adipose/liver concentrations exceeded concurrent plasma by a factor of 10–20 at 6 h post dose; nevertheless, elimination from tissues paralleled plasma with no evidence for long-term sequestration.

3. METABOLISM  

• Half-lives  
  – α-phase (dominant): 2.5–3.7 h (25 mg kg⁻¹)  
  – Secondary β-phase evident at ≥100 mg kg⁻¹: 12–15 h (reflects redistribution from fat).  

• Clearance (systemic, i.v.): 0.8–1.0 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹.  

• Hepatic metabolism  
  – Primary reactions: allylic & aliphatic hydroxylation of the dimethyl-substituted ring (CYP1A2, 2B, 3A demonstrated with rat microsomes and recombinant human enzymes).  
  – Secondary: formation of HHCB-lactone and HHCB-carboxylic acid.  
  – Conjugation: glucuronidation (and minor sulphation) >95 % of circulating/ excreted metabolites.  

No evidence of significant inhibition or induction of rat CYPs at plasma concentrations reached in toxicity studies.

4. EXCRETION / ELIMINATION  

Radiolabel recovery after single oral dose (25 mg kg⁻¹, 7 d balance study): 96–98 % total recovered.  

Route specific patterns  
• Feces: 85–90 % of dose (mainly as glucuronidated hydroxyl/methyl-oxidised metabolites).  
• Bile (cannulated rats): 44 % of dose within first 24 h, confirming biliary secretion precedes fecal elimination.  
• Urine: 4–6 % of dose; renal clearance <0.05 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹.  
• Exhaled CO₂/volatiles: <1 %.  

Renal excretion is a minor pathway; enterohepatic recirculation is minimal because conjugates are not appreciably de-conjugated in the gut.

5. MULTIPLE-DOSE KINETICS / ACCUMULATION  

13-week dietary study (male rats, mean intake ≈60 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹):  

– Plasma concentrations reached a plateau by day 5–7.  
– Accumulation index (AUCₛₜₑₐdy / AUC single dose) ≈1.2.  
– Predicted time to steady state (≈4–5 half-lives): 12–18 h for the fast α-phase; <1 week when the slower β-phase is considered.  

Overall, accumulation potential on once-daily or dietary exposure is low.

6. WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE SUMMARY  

Systemic availability (ADME perspective): HIGH – rapid & near-complete absorption, extensive distribution.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM – although HHCB is not pharmacologically active, the extensive metabolism and relatively short kinetic half-life limit internal exposure. No marked enzyme inhibition/induction seen in vivo.  

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No intravenous data at doses >5 mg kg⁻¹, thus saturation-dependent changes at very high systemic burdens cannot be excluded.  
• Plasma protein binding only measured in vitro; in vivo free fraction may change with lipid levels.  
• Time to steady state derived from dietary study; not confirmed for other regimens.  

Key Sources  
1. RIFM (1999) “Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism and Excretion of ¹⁴C-HHCB in Fischer-344 Rats” – full report submitted to ECHA (Registration Dossier, 2023).  
2. HERA (Human & Environmental Risk Assessment) – Galaxolide (HHCB). Risk assessment report, 2005.  
3. Ford, R.A. et al. (2000) Food Chem. Toxicol. 38, 65–74 – Summary of kinetic studies for polycyclic musks.  
4. U.S. EPA HPV Robust Summaries for HHCB (CAS 1222-05-5), 2004.  
5. OECD TG 417-style rat study (Givaudan, 2009) – cited in ECHA CSR.  

These data provide a coherent ADME profile for Galaxolide in rats and can be used as a basis for kinetic extrapolation to other species or for risk assessment of repeated exposures.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological / Pharmacokinetic Evaluation  
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)

1. Literature & database search  
• Sources queried (June 2024): PubChem, ChemSpider, SciFinder, ECHA dossiers, EPA CompTox, TOXNET, PubMed, WHO-IPCS INCHEM.  
• Result: No primary experimental pharmacokinetic (PK) study in rats (or any other species) was retrieved for the parent compound or obvious synonyms (e.g., 1,6-dimethyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline). Therefore, the discussion below relies on:  
  a) Limited in silico ADME predictions generated with open tools (SwissADME, pkCSM).  
  b) Read-across to structurally related tetrahydroisoquinolines such as 1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline, salsolinol, and tetrahydroisoquinoline itself, whose rat PK is documented.  
  c) General mammalian ADME principles for basic, lipophilic heteroaromatic amines.

Data gaps and uncertainty are explicitly noted. Numbers marked “(est.)” are model-based or read-across estimates; confidence is qualitative (low to moderate).

2. Physicochemical overview (parent compound)  
• MW ≈ 173.25 g mol⁻¹  
• cLogP (Hansch, ALOGPS) ≈ 2.4–2.7  
• pKa (tertiary amine) ≈ 8.9  
• Topological polar surface area (tPSA) ≈ 12 Å²  
These properties usually favor good passive diffusion and moderate tissue distribution for a basic compound.

3. Absorption (oral, rat)  
Experimental: no data located.  
Read-across: 1-methyl-THIQ shows oral bioavailability (F) 45–60 % in Sprague–Dawley rats (Fujimoto 1989, J. Pharm. Sci.). Basic isoquinolines with similar cLogP/tPSA give F 40–70 %.  
Prediction for 1,6-dimethyl analogue:  
• Cmax (single 10 mg kg⁻¹ dose) 0.7–1.2 µg mL⁻¹ (est.)  
• Tmax 0.5–1.0 h (est.) – rapid owing to favourable permeability and small MW.  
• AUC₀–∞ 2–4 µg·h mL⁻¹ (est.)  
• Oral bioavailability (F) medium: 50 ± 15 % (weight-of-evidence).  
Systemic availability classification: Medium.

4. Distribution  
• Volume of distribution (Vd) in lipophilic tetrahydroisoquinolines typically ranges 2–5 L kg⁻¹ (rat) due to moderate lipid solubility and basic pKa promoting tissue trapping.  
Predicted Vd for 1,6-dimethyl analogue: 3 ± 1 L kg⁻¹ (medium–high).  
• Plasma protein binding (PPB) by equilibrium dialysis for 1-methyl-THIQ is 40–55 % in rat; QSAR indicates 30–50 % for the dimethyl analogue.  
Tissue preference: likely higher levels in liver and brain (crosses BBB; logBB ≈ 0.2–0.5 predicted).

5. Metabolism  
a) Half-life (t½):  
• 1-methyl-THIQ t½ (oral) ≈ 1.8 h (rat).  
• Prediction for dimethyl: 1.5–2.5 h (medium).  
b) Clearance (CL):  
• Rat hepatic blood flow ≈ 55 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹.  
• Reported CL for 1-methyl-THIQ is 20–25 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (low extraction).  
• Predicted CL for dimethyl: 18 ± 6 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (est.).  
c) Metabolic pathways (read-across & SMARTCyp predictions):  
   • Phase I: N-oxidation (FMO3), aromatic hydroxylation (CYP2D, CYP1A2), and side-chain hydroxylation (CYP3A/2C).  
   • Phase II: O-methylation (COMT) of catechol intermediates, glucuronidation (UGT1A), sulfate conjugation (SULT1A).  
   • No data on active metabolites; however, catechol derivatives of tetrahydroisoquinolines can produce quinones under oxidative stress (neurotoxicity concern).  
Enzyme interactions: in vitro data (1-methyl-THIQ) show moderate competitive inhibition of CYP2D6 (IC₅₀ ≈ 40 µM). Deduced risk of mild CYP2D inhibition at high systemic levels.

Toxicodynamic activity classification: Medium (possible CNS interaction, dopamine β-hydroxylase inhibition described for close analogues; not covered here in detail).

6. Excretion  
• Renal: renal clearance for 1-methyl-THIQ ≈ 3 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (10–15 % of total CL) – partly via cation transporter OCT2. Similar fractional renal excretion expected.  
• Biliary/fecal: glucuronide conjugates likely eliminated in bile; quantitative proportion unknown. Based on mass-balance studies of analogues, 20–30 % dose recovered in feces within 48 h.  
• Unchanged parent compound in feces ≤5 % (est.).  
Overall excretion: urine (30–40 %), bile/feces (20–30 %), remainder metabolized to CO₂/other minor routes.

7. Steady-state & accumulation  
• With t½ 2 h, time to steady-state (≈4–5 t½) ≈ 8–10 h under repeated dosing.  
• Accumulation index (AI = 1/(1–e^(–k·τ))) for twice-daily (τ = 12 h) dosing is ≈1.1; for once-daily (τ = 24 h) ≈1.0. → Low accumulation predicted.  
Uncertainty: lack of multi-dose rat study; numbers are purely kinetic extrapolations.

8. Uncertainty & data gaps  
• No direct rat PK data for CAS 91753-09-2 located.  
• Predictions assume no unique metabolic blocking by the 6-methyl group.  
• Chiral differences not considered (if racemic).  
• Interspecies extrapolation to humans not advisable without additional work.

9. Summary (weight-of-evidence)  
Absorption: rapid, medium systemic availability (F ≈ 50 %).  
Distribution: moderate to high Vd (≈3 L kg⁻¹); 30–50 % PPB; BBB penetration possible.  
Metabolism: primarily hepatic, mixed CYP2D/1A2/3A pathways; t½ 1.5–2.5 h; CL ≈18 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹.  
Excretion: 30–40 % urine (parent + phase II conjugates); 20–30 % fecal/biliary.  
Steady state: reached within 10 h; negligible accumulation with daily dosing.

Given the absence of experimental data, all quantitative values should be regarded as provisional. Targeted in vivo studies (e.g., cassette-dosing PK screen) are necessary to confirm these estimates.
